column2: Details
Major Hekhalot text describing the ascent through the heavenly palaces (halls), angelic encounters, and the rituals and dangers of mystical ascent.

- Ascent through seven heavenly halls
- Angelic guardians and passwords
- Mystical hymns and prayers
- Perils of the unworthy mystic
Rabbi Ishmael, Rabbi Akiva, Rabbi Nehunya ben HaKanah
Descriptions of each palace/hall, angelic hierarchies, hymns recited by mystics
Composed c. 3rd–6th centuries CE, late antiquity, in the Land of Israel and Babylonia
Details
Later Hekhalot text presenting the transformation of Enoch into the angel Metatron and further elaborating on heavenly hierarchies and palaces.

- Enoch's ascent and transformation
- Angel Metatron
- Detailed angelic orders and roles
- Mystical cosmology
Rabbi Ishmael (visionary), Enoch/Metatron
Enoch's enthronement as Metatron, lists of angels and their functions
Composed c. 5th–6th centuries CE, probably in Babylonia

Hekhalot Rabbati (The Greater Palaces): Ma’aseh Merkavah (The Work of the Chariot)

column2: Details
Fragmentary Hekhalot text focusing on mystical hymns, prayers, and the practical techniques for the ascent to the Divine Chariot.

- Mystical hymns and prayers
- Rituals and passwords
- Visionary descriptions of the Chariot and angelic beings
Rabbi Akiva, Rabbi Ishmael, Rabbi Nehunya ben HaKanah
Hymns recited in the palaces, instructions for mystical ascent
Late Hellenistic to early post-Temple period (after 70 CE); part of early Merkabah literature1234
Details
Foundational medieval Kabbalistic text, presented as a mystical commentary on the Torah, especially Genesis and Exodus.

- Sefirot and divine emanations
- Mystical interpretation of Torah
- The soul’s journey
- The nature of the Divine Presence (Shekhinah)
Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai (attributed), his circle of disciples
The Idra Rabba and Idra Zuta (mystical assemblies), Sifra di-Tzeniuta, sections on the Holy of Holies
Composed in 13th-century Spain (medieval period); attributed pseudepigraphically to 2nd-century sages

Hekhalot Rabbati (The Greater Palaces): These tables provide a comparative overview of each major mystical text’s structure, content, and historical background, as well as the key figures associated with their transmission and teaching1248.
